title: SATBench: Benchmarking LLMs' Logical Reasoning via Automated Puzzle Generation from SAT Formulas
authors: Anjiang Wei, Yuheng Wu, Yingjia Wan, Tarun Suresh, Huanmi Tan, Zhanke Zhou, Sanmi Koyejo, Ke Wang, Alex Aiken (Stanford, UCLA, UIUC, CMU, Nanjing University)
venue: arXiv preprint (v1: 2025-05-20; v2: 2025-09-22). Note: the candidate dossier / corpus entry cites 'EMNLP 2025', but per the paper's own header/extraction notes, neither the arXiv HTML page nor its metadata contains an explicit acceptance/journal-ref line confirming EMNLP 2025 publication; this could not be independently verified from arXiv metadata alone.
claim: SATBench is a benchmark of 2100 logical puzzles automatically generated from Boolean satisfiability (SAT/CNF) formulas, translated into natural-language narrative scenarios by LLMs and validated by both solver-based and LLM-based consistency checks (plus human spot-checks). Because SAT is a search-based reasoning task (existence of a satisfying assignment, with both SAT and UNSAT outcomes possible) rather than an inference-rule-chaining task, the paper argues it tests a different and underexplored facet of logical reasoning than benchmarks like FOLIO/RuleTaker/PrOntoQA; even the strongest tested model (o4-mini) reaches only 65.0% accuracy on hard UNSAT problems, barely above the 50% random baseline.
novelty: The authors position SATBench's contribution against prior logical-reasoning benchmarks (Table 1) using a Search-Based column plus the paper's own explicitly named 'six criteria' (Table 1 caption: 'An ideal evaluation framework should meet the following six criteria'): (1) logic isolation, (2) fully automated/scalable generation, (3) adjustable difficulty (via clause count), (4) natural-language (not formal-symbol) presentation, (5) template-free diversity, and (6) evaluation of both prediction accuracy AND reasoning-trace validity. Search-Based is a separate, additional column in Table 1 (contrasting SATBench with inference-rule-based benchmarks like FOLIO/RuleTaker/P-FOLIO) that the paper's caption text does not enumerate among 'the six criteria,' even though SATBench is marked yes on it too. Across all seven Table 1 columns (Search-Based plus the six named criteria), SATBench is the only benchmark in the comparison table marked yes on every one. Relative to PARAT (Pan et al. 2024), which evaluates LLMs directly on SAT formulas, SATBench frames SAT as natural-language puzzles ('a more realistic setting given LLMs' training on text'). Relative to AutoLogi (Zhu et al. 2025), which builds on existing corpora and 'risks data contamination,' SATBench's puzzles are 'generated entirely from scratch with solver and human validation.' The paper also introduces reasoning-trace evaluation (LLM-as-judge checking whether the trace supports the SAT/UNSAT label, distinct from just scoring the label) and a systematic 4-way error taxonomy (satisfiability bias, context inconsistency, condition omission, spurious priors).

protocol.description: Zero-shot, single forward-pass prompting per puzzle instance for the main evaluation (Section 4, 'Prompts': 'We use 0-shot prompting to evaluate various LLMs on each logical puzzle in our dataset'). A second, separate single-shot call (with a different judge prompt, using GPT-4o as judge) evaluates the reasoning trace's validity post hoc, but this is not an interactive/multi-turn loop with the model under test -- it is a one-shot generation followed by an independent one-shot judgment of that same generation's trace.
protocol.observation_content: The model under test sees, in one call, the full puzzle: a <scenario> (narrative background), a <conditions> list (one natural-language sentence per CNF clause), and a <question> asking about satisfiability -- no demos/few-shot examples are used (0-shot). Exact verbatim prompt template (Appendix B.2 / Figure A3): 'You are a logical reasoning assistant. You are given a logic puzzle. <scenario>{scenario} <conditions>{conditions} <question>{question} Guidelines: - All constraints come **only** from the <conditions> section. - The <scenario> provides background and intuition, but **does not impose any additional rules or constraints**. - All variables represent **independent decisions**; there is no mutual exclusivity or implicit linkage unless stated explicitly in <conditions>. - Variables not mentioned in <conditions> are considered unknown and irrelevant to satisfiability. Your task: - If the puzzle is satisfiable, propose one valid assignment that satisfies all the conditions. - If the puzzle is unsatisfiable, explain why some of the conditions cannot all be true at once. Think step by step. At the end of your answer, output exactly one of the following labels on a new line: [SAT] - if a valid assignment exists [UNSAT] - if the constraints cannot be satisfied Do not add any text or formatting after the final label.' The model is required to produce a chain-of-thought reasoning trace plus a final [SAT]/[UNSAT] label; no external tool/solver access is permitted for the model under test ('we use chain-of-thought prompting and prohibit models from invoking external tools; solvers are used only during dataset generation for validation', Section 2). A separate judge LLM (GPT-4o) is then shown the puzzle plus the model's trace plus the ground-truth formula (and, for UNSAT, the UNSAT-core) in a second single-shot call to score trace validity (Appendix B.3, Figures A4/A5).

representations.variants_compared: Section 6.1 ('SAT in Natural Language') directly compares two input representations: (1) the natural-language narrative puzzle (scenario + conditions + question) and (2) the raw CNF/SAT formula given directly to the model.
representations.effect_on_accuracy: Table 6: accuracy was consistently HIGHER on raw SAT-formula inputs than on narrative puzzles for every tested model -- GPT-4o-mini 53.6% (puzzle) vs 54.8% (formula); GPT-4o 58.2% vs 59.2%; DeepSeek-V3 84.0% vs 87.3%; o4-mini 89.4% vs 94.3%. The paper's conclusion: 'natural language introduces additional complexity and makes the benchmark more challenging' (Section 6.1) -- i.e., the surface-form/representation choice is a real difficulty dial, and moving from symbolic to natural-language framing costs several accuracy points even holding the underlying logic fixed.

models.name: o4-mini
models.key_results: Table 3: overall avg accuracy 89.3% (highest of all models tested); Overall-Hard 78.0%; UNSAT-Hard 65.0% (near the 50% random baseline). Table 4: SAT-Pred 94.7%, SAT-Trace 74.6%, UNSAT-Pred 83.6%, UNSAT-Trace 74.1%, Overall-Trace 74.4%. Table 6: puzzle-form accuracy 89.4% vs formula-form 94.3%. Table 5 error breakdown: Satisfiability Bias 68.7%, Context Inconsistency 17.0%, Condition Omission 14.3%, Spurious Priors 0.0%. Section 6.2: error-aware prompting corrected 60.4% of previously-failing cases.

models.name: DeepSeek-R1
models.key_results: Table 3: overall avg 87.8% (second highest). Table 4: SAT-Pred 85.2%, SAT-Trace 73.8%, UNSAT-Pred 90.3%, UNSAT-Trace 82.1%, Overall-Trace 78.0%. Table 5 errors: Satisfiability Bias 40.5%, Context Inconsistency 44.7%, Condition Omission 12.6%, Spurious Priors 2.1%. Section 6.2: error-aware prompting corrected 73.2% of previously-failing cases; its 1100 correct traces were also used as fine-tuning data for a smaller model.

models.name: DeepSeek-V3
models.key_results: Table 3: overall avg 84.0% (third highest). Table 4: SAT-Pred 82.9%, SAT-Trace 65.7%, UNSAT-Pred 85.0%, UNSAT-Trace 71.1%, Overall-Trace 68.4%. Table 6: puzzle 84.0% vs formula 87.3%.

models.name: Claude-3.7-Sonnet
models.key_results: Table 3: overall avg 74.8% (4th of 21 rows); Overall-Hard 62.9%. Table 4: SAT-Pred 83.2%, SAT-Trace 47.4%, UNSAT-Pred 66.4%, UNSAT-Trace 61.1%, Overall-Trace 54.2%.

models.name: GPT-4o / GPT-4o-mini
strategies: also used as the puzzle-generation and validation/judge model (not just a test subject)
models.key_results: Table 3: gpt-4o overall avg 58.0%; gpt-4o-mini overall avg 53.9% (near-random). Table 6: gpt-4o puzzle 58.2% vs formula 59.2%; gpt-4o-mini puzzle 53.6% vs formula 54.8%. GPT-4o is also the model used for LLM-based consistency validation (Section 3.3) and as the judge for reasoning-trace correctness (Section 4, Metrics).

models.name: Open-source model families (Qwen3, LLaMA3.1/3.3/4, Mixtral, DeepSeek-Distill, QwQ)
strategies: scaling comparison across model sizes within a family
models.key_results: Table 3 gives per-model overall avg from 38.2% (LLaMA3.1-8B) up to 68.3% (Qwen-QwQ-32B). Figure 3 / Section 5.1 'Scaling Trends': larger models within a family generally score higher overall, but 'on the hard instances, accuracy plateaus around 50-53% even for the largest models in these families' -- scaling gains are concentrated in easy problems and largely absent on hard instances.

models.name: Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct (fine-tuning target)
strategies: LoRA supervised fine-tuning on 1100 o4-mini-generated correct traces
models.key_results: Section 6.2 'Fine-tuning': accuracy raised from 51.9% to 53.6% -- 'modest relative to prompting, this indicates that supervised fine-tuning can help, with greater gains expected from larger datasets and reinforcement learning.'

headline_numbers: o4-mini achieves 65.0% accuracy on hard UNSAT problems, 'close to the random baseline of 50%' (Abstract; Section 5.1; Table 3, UNSAT-Hard column).
o4-mini's overall accuracy on Hard instances (SAT+UNSAT combined) is 78.0%, 'only a moderate improvement over the 50% random baseline' (Section 5.1; Table 3, Overall-Hard column).
Across all 20 tested models, average accuracy on hard problems is 53.0%, 'nearly equivalent to the random baseline' (Section 5.1; Table 3, Overall-Hard row 'Average').
SAT vs UNSAT asymmetry: average hard-instance accuracy is 66.7% on SAT vs only 39.3% on UNSAT (below the 50% random baseline) (Section 5.2 'SAT versus UNSAT'; Table 3).
Natural-language framing costs accuracy vs. raw formula input across all 4 models tested in the ablation, e.g. o4-mini 89.4% (puzzle) vs 94.3% (formula) (Section 6.1; Table 6).
Error-aware prompting corrects a majority of previously-failing cases: 60.4% for o4-mini, 73.2% for DeepSeek-R1 (Section 6.2 'Prompting').
LoRA fine-tuning on 1100 correct o4-mini traces raises Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct accuracy from 51.9% to 53.6% (Section 6.2 'Fine-tuning').

Dataset scale/composition: 2100 instances, average 36.0 boolean variables, 20.6 clauses, 546.2 words, 55.2 sentences per puzzle (Table 2); difficulty bins are Easy (4-19 clauses), Medium (20-30), Hard (31-50) (Table 3 caption).
failure_modes: Satisfiability Bias: 'Models answer SAT but give an invalid assignment' -- the model outputs a partial/invalid assignment and prematurely declares satisfiability without checking all clauses, i.e. failing to do exhaustive search/backtracking (Section 5.3; Table 5: 68.7% of o4-mini's errors, 40.5% of DeepSeek-R1's errors; worked example in Appendix C).

Context Inconsistency: 'Models contradict their earlier reasoning, such as assigning conflicting values across steps' -- e.g. assigning x(0)=1 then later x(0)=0 within the same trace (Section 5.3; Table 5: 17.0% o4-mini, 44.7% DeepSeek-R1; Appendix C).

Condition Omission: 'Models ignore one or more conditions in the reasoning trace,' e.g. dropping a disjunct and wrongly concluding UNSAT on an actually-satisfiable formula (Section 5.3; Table 5: 14.3% o4-mini, 12.6% DeepSeek-R1; Appendix C).

Spurious Priors: 'Models introduce commonsense assumptions that are absent from the given constraints,' e.g. assuming two disjuncts are mutually exclusive when they are not, causing an incorrect UNSAT conclusion (Section 5.3; Table 5: 0.0% o4-mini, 2.1% DeepSeek-R1; Appendix C).

Trace-vs-label disconnect: 'higher prediction accuracy on SAT problems does not necessarily imply a valid variable assignment. Instead, models often show a bias toward predicting SAT outcomes without a valid assignment as evidence' -- trace validity drops more sharply than label accuracy on the SAT side (Section 5.3, 'Trace Evaluation'; Table 4).

Clause-count sensitivity: accuracy shows 'a noticeable inverse relationship' with clause count, e.g. GPT-4o 'experiences a significant drop in performance, nearing random guess accuracy of 50% as it approaches 30 clauses' (Section 5.2, 'Impact of Clause Quantity'; Figure 4).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper's core evaluation is entirely single forward-pass (0-shot, no demonstrations, no multi-turn interaction with the model under test): 'We use 0-shot prompting to evaluate various LLMs on each logical puzzle in our dataset' (Section 4, 'Prompts'). Within that one pass, models are asked for CoT reasoning plus a final label, but this is not an interactive loop -- there is no feedback returned to the model and no second attempt. The paper does not test few-shot or many-shot demonstration prompting as an ablation; the closest thing to 'more demonstrations helping' is Section 6.2's two post hoc improvement studies: (1) error-aware prompting -- a revised 0-shot prompt (still single-shot, no interaction) that explicitly names the four failure patterns discovered via error analysis, which corrects 60.4% (o4-mini) / 73.2% (DeepSeek-R1) of previously-wrong cases; and (2) LoRA fine-tuning on 1100 correct reasoning traces from o4-mini, raising a smaller model's accuracy only modestly (51.9% -> 53.6%), described as 'modest relative to prompting.' The paper's own conclusion is that within a single forward pass, models -- even 'reasoning' models like o4-mini -- systematically fail to perform the backtracking/exhaustive-search process required for hard SAT/UNSAT instances ('failures in backtracking, difficulty in maintaining context, and reliance on prior knowledge rather than provided constraints', Section 5.3), and that surfacing these failure categories explicitly in the prompt (still single-shot) recovers a large fraction of errors, while weight updates from correct traces help only marginally at the scale tested.

relation_to_candidate.position: SATBench is single-shot / one-forward-pass only (0-shot, no demos, no interactive/multi-turn loop with the model under test) -- the model sees the full puzzle (scenario + conditions + question) in one call and must emit a CoT trace plus a final [SAT]/[UNSAT] label in that same call. What is observed by the model under test is the complete problem statement; nothing is withheld or revealed incrementally. Output shape is a single binary label (SAT vs. UNSAT), not a full derivation/sequence -- though the paper does separately (in a second, judge-only call) grade whether the accompanying free-text reasoning trace is valid, using an LLM-as-judge verifier rather than a symbolic/programmatic one for that secondary check. The primary label-correctness verifier IS however a solver oracle: ground truth SAT/UNSAT comes from an actual SAT solver used at generation time, and equivalence between the original and reconstructed CNF is solver-checked. c18, by contrast, centers on chaining/entailment (True/False/Unknown over a theory of facts+if-then rules) with depth as the core dial, whereas SATBench centers on constraint satisfiability (search for an assignment) with clause-count as the core dial and both directions of outcome (SAT/UNSAT) rather than the entailment/non-entailment/unknown framing.
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c18: Both are (a) synthetic, fully-automated generators with a verifiable ground-truth oracle (SATBench: SAT solver; c18: prontoqa's proof engine) that make difficulty an explicit, tunable dial (SATBench: clause count -> Easy/Medium/Hard bins; c18: proof depth D0-D5); (b) single-call, single-label classification tasks scored by exact match against an automatically-generated gold label (SATBench: SAT/UNSAT; c18: True/False/Unknown); (c) designed to be contamination-resistant via fresh instance generation per run (SATBench regenerates from sampled CNF + fresh narrative scenarios each time; c18 uses fresh fictional predicates per seed); (d) both report a documented 'hard tail' where accuracy approaches the random/majority baseline even for the strongest model (SATBench: o4-mini 65.0% UNSAT-Hard vs. 50% random; c18's PrOntoQA-OOD lineage documents a similar depth-scaling ceiling); (e) both surface a qualitative error taxonomy tied to reasoning-process failures (SATBench's 4 error types -- satisfiability bias, context inconsistency, condition omission, spurious priors -- are close in spirit to the kinds of chaining/negation-as-failure errors the c18 family targets).
relation_to_candidate.differences: (1) Task semantics: SATBench is a search/CSP problem (does a satisfying assignment exist -- existential over an unbounded assignment space, admitting a genuine UNSAT branch), whereas c18 is a deductive-chaining/entailment problem (does the query provably follow via forward-chaining over stated rules, with closed/open-world Unknown as the third option) -- the paper itself frames this contrast explicitly (Section 1, Section 6.1) as distinguishing 'search-based' from 'inference rule-based' reasoning, citing FOLIO/RuleTaker/P-FOLIO (c18's direct lineage) as the inference-rule-based comparison class it is NOT emulating. (2) Difficulty dial: clause/variable count (SATBench) vs. proof depth plus distractor rules and negation-as-failure/open-world semantics (c18). (3) Output/verifier: SATBench's primary check is solver-verified label correctness (objective, external oracle) plus a SEPARATE LLM-judged free-text trace-validity score (subjective, model-based judge) as a secondary metric; c18's proposed design explicitly drops full proof-chain output to keep only the single T/F/Unknown label for exact-match scoring, and per the c18 measurement-skeptic notes, that label is itself 'definitional' (from a negation flag) rather than independently re-derived unless a forward-chaining fixpoint oracle is added -- i.e., SATBench already has (and SATBench's design already required) an independent solver oracle by construction, something the c18 implementation plan flags as a to-do risk to mitigate. (4) Representation ablation: SATBench directly measures natural-language-vs-formula framing as a difficulty dial (Table 6, Section 6.1) with 4 models; c18's dossier as read gives no equivalent NL-vs-symbolic ablation. (5) Model coverage and prompting scope: SATBench evaluates 20+ proprietary and open-source models with a scaling-trend study (Figure 3) and post hoc improvement experiments (error-aware prompting, LoRA fine-tuning); the c18 dossier content available here does not include a comparable multi-model or improvement-intervention study for c18 itself.
relation_to_candidate.what_c18_would_add: c18 would add: (a) a genuine multi-hop deductive-chaining stratum with an explicit, orthogonal depth ladder (D0-D5) rather than clause-count as the only knob, letting depth and rule/distractor composition be varied independently of instance size; (b) the closed-world/open-world Unknown three-way distinction, which SATBench does not have (SATBench is strictly binary SAT/UNSAT); (c) a proof-engine oracle that names the specific failed inference step for reflection diagnostics, which is a finer-grained ground-truth artifact than SATBench's UNSAT-core (SATBench's core is a minimal unsatisfiable clause subset, useful for judging trace validity, but not tied to a step-by-step forward-chaining derivation the way c18's proof engine is); (d) per the c18 dossier's own design notes, a second CSP/unique-solution seating-puzzle stratum, which is structurally close to (and could reuse ideas from) SATBench's search-based CNF-to-narrative framing -- suggesting SATBench is a natural reference point/hard-tail complement for c18's CSP stratum rather than a competitor for its core chaining stratum.

corpus_claim: SATBench (Wei et al., 2025) is the recent hard tail for c18: a SAT/CNF-to-puzzle generator with a solver oracle and documented large headroom (o4-mini near random on hard UNSAT). (Note: the corpus says EMNLP 2025, but acquisition could not confirm any venue from arXiv metadata -- check what the paper itself supports.)
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported (with a venue miscitation flag). The substantive claim is supported by the paper: it IS a SAT/CNF-to-puzzle generator (Section 3), it DOES have a solver oracle (SAT-solver-based consistency validation, Section 3.3, and solver-derived ground-truth SAT/UNSAT labels, Section 3.4), and it DOES document large headroom on hard UNSAT problems for the best model -- o4-mini scores 65.0% on UNSAT-Hard, 'close to the random baseline of 50%' (Abstract; Table 3). However, the venue portion of the claim ('EMNLP 2025') is NOT supported by the paper's own header/metadata: per the extraction's own discrepancy note, 'Neither the arXiv HTML page nor its metadata contains an explicit acceptance/journal-ref line confirming EMNLP 2025 publication.' The only EMNLP references in the document are citations to OTHER papers (e.g., Olausson et al., Saeed et al.), not a self-referential venue tag. So the paper itself supports the empirical/technical content of the corpus claim but does not itself substantiate the EMNLP 2025 venue attribution -- that detail should be treated as unverified/possibly-miscited pending direct confirmation against EMNLP 2025 proceedings or the ACL Anthology.
baseline_relevant: Instance scale/difficulty dials: 2100 total puzzles; average 36.0 variables and 20.6 clauses per instance (Table 2); difficulty bins defined purely by clause count: Easy 4-19, Medium 20-30, Hard 31-50 clauses (Table 3 caption). Average puzzle length is substantial: 546.2 words / 55.2 sentences (Table 2) -- i.e. narrative framing inflates token length well beyond the underlying formula.

Prompt format matters a lot: natural-language narrative framing is measurably harder than giving the raw CNF formula directly, for every one of the 4 models tested in the ablation (Table 6; e.g. o4-mini 89.4% puzzle vs 94.3% formula) -- a concrete, quantified representation effect worth mirroring/testing for c18's own surface-form choices.

Metric: primary metric is simple exact-match accuracy on a binary (SAT/UNSAT) label, with random baseline fixed at 50% (Section 4, 'Metrics'); a secondary trace-validity metric is computed only conditional on the label being correct, using an LLM-as-judge scored [YES]/[NO] (Section 3.4, Table 4).

Floors/ceilings observed: best-model ceiling on hard UNSAT is 65.0% (o4-mini), barely above the 50% floor; average-model floor on all hard instances is 53.0% (Table 3) -- demonstrating clause-count is an effective difficulty dial that drives some models below random on UNSAT-Hard specifically (e.g., gpt-4o-mini UNSAT-Hard = 13.2%, well below 50%, Table 3) despite performing far above random on SAT-Hard (90.7%) -- a stark reminder that aggregate/overall accuracy can mask a severe asymmetric floor hidden in one branch of a binary task.

Contamination/data-release status: dataset and code are publicly released (https://github.com/Anjiang-Wei/SATBench) and explicitly designed to be regenerable ('The dataset generation process is fully automated, allowing for the creation of additional instances as required,' Section 4) -- directly analogous to c18's own regeneration-based contamination-resistance strategy, meaning any reuse of the *released* 2100 instances would carry the same contamination risk the c18 design explicitly avoids by regenerating with fresh seeds/symbols.

Validation protocol worth reusing for baseline design: three-pronged consistency validation -- LLM-based clause-matching check, solver-based bidirectional-entailment equivalence check, and human validation on a 100-instance random sample, reporting near-ceiling pass rates (100% scenario/mapping consistency, 97% clause-translation faithfulness, 93% LLM-judgment correctness; Section 5.4) -- a useful template for how much/what kind of human auditing to budget when standing up a new synthetic generator.

Improvement levers and their relative size, useful for calibrating what 'a strong baseline' should try before declaring headroom: error-aware (still single-shot) prompting recovered 60.4%-73.2% of previously-wrong cases (Section 6.2), while LoRA fine-tuning on 1100 correct traces moved a 14B open model only 51.9% -> 53.6% -- i.e., prompt engineering closed far more of the gap than fine-tuning did at this data scale, worth noting when deciding where to invest baseline-improvement effort for c18.
quotes: "We use 0-shot prompting to evaluate various LLMs on each logical puzzle in our dataset." (Section 4, Prompts)
"Experimental results show that even the strongest model, o4-mini, achieves only 65.0% accuracy on hard UNSAT problems, close to the random baseline of 50%." (Abstract)
"For the hard instances, even the state-of-the-art model o4-mini only achieves 78.0% accuracy, only a moderate improvement over the 50% random baseline." (Section 5.1, Main Results)
"Models perform better on SAT problems, achieving an accuracy of 66.7% on the hard instances, while only reaching 39.3% on hard UNSAT problems." (Section 5.2, SAT versus UNSAT)

"As shown in Table 6, accuracy was consistently higher on raw SAT inputs than on narrative puzzles, showing that natural language introduces additional complexity and makes the benchmark more challenging." (Section 6.1, SAT in Natural Language)

"Re-evaluating the previously misclassified cases under this setting led to substantial gains: for o4-mini, 60.4% of failing cases were corrected, and for DeepSeek-R1, the rate is 73.2%." (Section 6.2, Prompting)
"Using 1100 correct traces from o4-mini, we applied LoRA fine-tuning on Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct, raising accuracy from 51.9% to 53.6%." (Section 6.2, Fine-tuning)

"Neither the arXiv HTML page nor its metadata contains an explicit acceptance/journal-ref line confirming EMNLP 2025 publication." (Header discrepancy note in the local extraction file, 2505.14615-satbench.md)

corrections: protocol.observation_content: the 'verbatim' quote of the Appendix B.2 / Figure A3 prompt template had a fabricated period inserted after '[UNSAT] - if the constraints cannot be satisfied' (source has no period there before 'Do not add any text...'); removed the spurious period to restore true verbatim fidelity.

novelty: the dossier miscounted Table 1's criteria, describing 'search-based framing' as one of the paper's own explicitly enumerated 'six criteria.' The paper's Table 1 caption defines exactly six named criteria (Logic Isolation, Automated Generation, Difficulty Control, Natural Language, Template-Free, Reasoning Evaluation) and treats Search-Based as a separate, additional column not included in that enumerated list (even though SATBench also scores yes on it). Rewrote to correctly separate the Search-Based column from the paper's own named six criteria while preserving the substantive claim that SATBench is the only benchmark checking every column.

corpus_claim_verdict: fabricated citation examples -- the dossier attributed 'Han et al. 2024b P-FOLIO, Saeed et al. RuleBERT' as the source markdown's own example EMNLP-venue citations, but the source's actual discrepancy note (line 16 of 2505.14615-satbench.md) names 'Olausson et al., Saeed et al.' as its examples, not P-FOLIO/RuleBERT. Corrected the quoted examples to match the source verbatim.
